hero.title: Flood Exposure Story Map For Myanmar, Thailand
hero.subtitle: In disaster risk management, knowing where the water flows is only half the challenge; knowing who is in its path is what saves lives. This guide explains how we turn complex flood models into actionable humanitarian insights. Effective humanitarian response requires anticipating the human cost of a disaster. This Story Map breaks down how we combine global flood simulations with demographic data to identify vulnerable communities before the water rises

sidebar.title: Flood Exposure
sidebar.subtitle: Understand how flood exposure indicators are produced and what they mean.

steps.title: Choose a flood scenario
steps.body: We use modelled flood inundation data from the JRC **Global Flood Awareness System (GloFAS)**

steps.title: Apply the flood-depth threshold
steps.body:
We only count areas where modelled flood depth is above a defined threshold.

In this example: above **30 cm**.

steps.title: Overlay population or facilities
steps.body: The flood layer is overlaid with population or facility datasets (such as WorldPop 2020) to identify what is located in the flooded area.

steps.title: Focus on a population group
steps.body: We can calculate exposure for specific vulnerable groups. In this example: **children under 5 years**.

steps.title: Aggregate to administrative areas
steps.body: Results are aggregated to the **ADM2 (district)** level.

aside.title: About this story map
aside.body: This page explains the concept behind flood exposure indicators. Numbers are examples for illustration only.

header.title: The Core Concept: What does “children under 5 exposed to a 50-year flood event above 30 cm” mean ?
header.description: The Concept: What does it actually mean when we say  “Children under 5 exposed to a 50-year flood event above 30 cm”? It sounds complex, but it’s simply a combination of Hazard (where the water goes) + Population (who is there). Let's break down how we calculate this, step by step, without the GIS jargon. It estimates how many children under 5 are living in areas that could be flooded with more than 30 cm of water during a flood that has a 1% chance of being exceeded in any given year. To make this actionable, we combine three things: Flood Hazard + Population = Exposed Population

note.body: “50-year return period flood” does not mean it happens exactly once every 50 years. It means there is a 1% chance of a flood this size being exceeded in any given year. Naturally, a 50-year flood covers a larger area than a 10-year flood, but a smaller area than 100- or 500-year events.
Depth matters for survival and logistics. Only grid cells with a flood depth strictly above 30 cm are considered in this indicator, filtering out areas with minor water accumulation.
By stacking the hazard map over the demographic grid, we pinpoint exactly where human settlements intersect with dangerous water levels.
Humanitarian resources are limited and must be prioritized. Only the selected population group located within that specific exposed area is counted.

Pixel-level data is hard to use for operational planning. By grouping the numbers (e.g., District A: 4,200 exposed vs. District D: 320 exposed), we allow decision-makers to easily compare regions and allocate aid where it is needed most.

callouts.title: What this indicator tells us
callouts.body: It provides a calculated estimate of how many children under 5 are living in areas that could experience more than 30 cm of flood water during a major event (1% annual chance). This is based on globally consistent modelled flood data and population estimates, allowing for fair regional comparisons.

callouts.title: What this indicator does not tell us
items: It does not predict exactly when or if the flood will actually occur.
It does not account for building quality, elevation of specific buildings, or local protection measures like flood walls.
It does not reflect informal local drainage structures or the effectiveness of early warning systems.
Most importantly, it shows potential exposure, not actual physical impacts, injuries, or damages.
